1.synthesis: Paul opens with Sosthenes to "the church of God that is in Corinth, to those sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints together with all those who in every place call upon the name of our Lord Jesus Christ" (vv.1–2), and wishes them "grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" (v.3). In a salutation addressed to a fractured, status-obsessed congregation, Paul packs the foundations of his whole appeal: he is a "called" apostle, they are "sanctified" and "called" saints, and their identity is bound up with the wider church that calls on Christ as Lord. Before a word of correction, he grounds them in grace.
1.historical_context: The opening follows the standard Greek letter form (sender, recipient, greeting) but Paul fills it with theology: his apostleship is "by the will of God" (v.1), divinely commissioned, not self-appointed. Sosthenes may be the synagogue ruler beaten before Gallio (Acts 18:17), now a believer. The word "church" (ekklēsia, v.2) carried a double resonance for Corinthians — the Septuagint's term for the assembly of Israel and the Greek civic citizen-assembly. "Sanctified" is a perfect participle, a completed consecration defining their present standing, and "call upon the name of our Lord Jesus Christ" applies to Jesus the Old Testament language of calling on the name of the LORD (cf. Joel 2:32).
1.literary_structure: The salutation quietly previews the letter's themes. "Called… called" (vv.1–2) and "sanctified" set divine initiative against Corinthian self-promotion, the keynote of chapters 1–4. The reminder that they are saints "together with all those who in every place" call on Christ (v.2) subtly rebukes a congregation prone to factional isolation, locating them within the universal church. "Grace… and peace" (v.3) is Paul's signature fusion of the Greek chairein and the Hebrew shalom, both flowing jointly "from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ."
1.theological_application: Paul addresses a deeply troubled church first as those "sanctified in Christ Jesus" and "saints" (v.2) — establishing their identity in Christ before confronting their sins, the gracious pattern of all gospel correction. Holiness is here a status conferred, not an achievement earned, the secure ground from which growth is then called for. And the reminder that they belong with "all who… call upon the name of our Lord" (v.2) lifts the believer's eyes beyond the local quarrel to the one worldwide people of God.
1.christology: The greeting is saturated with Christ: Paul is his apostle, the church is sanctified "in Christ Jesus," and believers are those who "call upon the name of our Lord Jesus Christ" (vv.1–2) — a worship-confession that applies the divine name to Jesus, an early and unselfconscious testimony to his deity. Grace and peace come jointly "from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" (v.3), setting the Son alongside the Father as the divine source of every blessing. The whole letter's authority rests on this Christological footing.

4.synthesis: Paul gives thanks for the Corinthians, "because of the grace of God that was given you in Christ Jesus," for in him "you were enriched in every way—in all speech and all knowledge" so that "you are not lacking in any gift, as you wait for the revealing of our Lord Jesus Christ" (vv.4–7). He assures them that God "will sustain you to the end, guiltless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ," for "God is faithful, by whom you were called into the fellowship of his Son" (vv.8–9). Even to a church he must rebuke, Paul gives sincere thanks — but his thanks subtly reframes their prized gifts as grace received and points them to the coming day.
4.historical_context: Thanksgivings were conventional in ancient letters, but Paul fills his with theology, grounding it "because of the grace of God" (chariti, v.4) — the initiating divine act, not Corinthian attainment. He singles out their enrichment "in all speech and all knowledge" (v.5), precisely the rhetorical and intellectual endowments this status-conscious congregation prized and competed over (and which chapters 1–4, 8, and 12–14 will address). The reference to "every gift" (charisma, v.7) anticipates the extended treatment of spiritual gifts, and "the revealing of our Lord Jesus Christ" (v.7) sets the eschatological horizon — the Parousia that will also be the day of judgment (v.8).
4.literary_structure: This thanksgiving is shrewdly worded to serve the letter's argument. By thanking God for the Corinthians' "speech and knowledge" as grace-gifts (v.5), Paul affirms what is genuine while quietly relocating its source in God, defusing their boasting before he confronts it. The repeated "day of our Lord Jesus Christ" (v.8) reorients a community with an over-realized sense of having already arrived toward a future still awaited. The closing affirmation "God is faithful" (v.9) introduces the note of divine reliability that undergirds the whole letter and leads directly into the appeal for unity in v.10.
4.theological_application: Every spiritual gift is grace received, not merit earned (v.4) — a truth that cuts the root of all spiritual pride: there is nothing to boast in that was not given. The believer's final security rests not on personal faithfulness but on God's: "he will sustain you to the end, guiltless" because "God is faithful" (vv.8–9). This is bedrock assurance for the struggling Christian — the One who called will keep. And the gifts are given to a people "waiting" (v.7): present endowments serve a future hope, not present status.
4.christology: The whole thanksgiving is Christ-centered: grace is given "in Christ Jesus," enrichment comes through him, and the goal is the "revealing" and "day" of "our Lord Jesus Christ" (vv.4–8). Believers are "called into the fellowship of his Son" (v.9) — communion with Jesus himself is the very purpose of their calling. That Christ will keep them "guiltless in the day of our Lord" (v.8) presents him as both the coming Judge and the ground of the believer's acquittal before that judgment.

10.synthesis: Paul turns at once to the crisis: "that there be no divisions among you, but that you be united in the same mind and the same judgment" (v.10). Chloe's people have reported quarreling, with each saying, "I follow Paul," "I follow Apollos," "I follow Cephas," "I follow Christ" (vv.11–12). Paul shatters the factions with three questions: "Is Christ divided? Was Paul crucified for you? Or were you baptized in the name of Paul?" (v.13). He is glad he baptized few, for Christ sent him "not to baptize but to preach the gospel, and not with words of eloquent wisdom, lest the cross of Christ be emptied of its power" (v.17). The cross, not the eloquence of leaders, is the church's true center.
10.historical_context: Corinth was a Roman colony driven by social climbing, patron-client bonds, and competitive status display, and the factions reflect this: clients publicly attached themselves to powerful figures (v.12). Apollos was "eloquent" and Alexandrian-trained (Acts 18:24), and the Corinthian preference for him reflects the Greek premium on rhetorical education. Chloe (v.11) is a woman heading a household large enough to send representatives between cities — early evidence of women's prominence in Pauline churches. The word "united" (katērtismenoi, v.10) is the term for mending nets or setting a broken bone: the church needs repair. Baptism "into" a name (v.13) created an allegiance-bond, which is why Paul denies the factions any legitimacy.
10.literary_structure: This passage states the problem that chapters 1–4 address — partisan rivalry around leaders — and frames it as fundamentally about the cross versus worldly wisdom. The three rhetorical questions of v.13 demolish the factions by appeal to what is unique to Christ: only he was crucified for them, only into his name were they baptized. The pivot to "eloquent wisdom" (v.17) is the hinge that launches the great cross-versus-wisdom argument of vv.18–2:16. The phrase "lest the cross be emptied of its power" sets human rhetoric and the cross in opposition, the theme that governs the whole section.
10.theological_application: Christian unity is grounded in shared allegiance to the crucified Christ, not in personalities: to rally around a favorite leader is to fracture what belongs to Christ alone (v.13). The appeal "that there be no divisions" (v.10) rebukes the perennial church temptation to form parties around teachers, traditions, or styles. Paul's deliberate refusal of "eloquent wisdom" (v.17) warns that even good gifts like persuasive speech become idols when they displace the cross, and that the gospel's power lies in its message, not its packaging.
10.christology: "Was Paul crucified for you?" (v.13) presses the uniqueness of Christ's saving death: he alone died for sinners, so he alone commands the church's undivided loyalty. The fear that the cross might be "emptied of its power" (v.17) reveals Paul's conviction that the crucifixion of Christ is itself the power of God, not a defeat to be dressed up in rhetoric. Every faction and every reliance on human wisdom is, at root, a failure to grasp that the crucified Christ is the sole foundation and center of the people of God.

18.synthesis: Paul now states the paradox at the heart of the gospel: "the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God" (v.18). God has chosen to "destroy the wisdom of the wise" (v.19), so that "the world did not know God through wisdom" but is saved through "the folly of what we preach" (vv.20–21). Jews demand signs and Greeks seek wisdom, "but we preach Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Gentiles" — yet to the called, "Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God" (vv.22–24). For "the foolishness of God is wiser than men, and the weakness of God is stronger than men" (v.25).
18.historical_context: Crucifixion was the most shameful death in the Roman world, reserved for slaves and rebels; Cicero said the very word "cross" should be kept far from a Roman citizen's eyes and ears, and Jewish sensibility added the curse on anyone "hung on a tree" (Deuteronomy 21:23). So a crucified Savior was "folly" (mōria) to Greeks and a "stumbling block" (skandalon) to Jews (v.23) — a category-violation of both the Jewish hope for a victorious king and the Greek ideal of the powerful, beautiful hero. Paul cites Isaiah 29:14 (v.19), where God overturns the cleverness of Jerusalem's advisors, applying the same pattern to Corinth's wisdom-competition. "Jews demand signs and Greeks seek wisdom" (v.22) names the two cultural appetites the cross confounds.
18.literary_structure: This is the core of the argument launched at v.17: a sustained meditation on the cross as God's reversal of human values, built on a series of paradoxes — folly that is power, weakness that is strength, the foolishness of God wiser than men (v.25). The structure pivots on the contrast between "those who are perishing" and "those who are being saved" (v.18), and between human expectation (signs, wisdom) and divine action (Christ crucified, vv.22–23). The Isaiah citation frames the whole as the continuation of God's age-old way of confounding the wise, setting up the sociological turn of vv.26–31.
18.theological_application: The gospel cannot be received on the world's terms; the cross is only legible as "power" and "wisdom" to those being saved (v.18, v.24), so faith requires the humbling of human pretension. This rebukes every attempt to make Christianity respectable by the standards of cultural sophistication or spectacular proof (v.22). It also comforts: the message that looks like weakness and folly to the world is in fact the saving power of God, so believers need not be ashamed of a gospel the world despises. God deliberately saves in a way that leaves no room for human boasting.
18.christology: Christ crucified is here identified as "the power of God and the wisdom of God" (v.24): the very attributes the Greeks sought in philosophy and the Jews in mighty signs are found in the shameful death of Jesus. The cross is not a defeat God later reverses but is itself the place where his power and wisdom are most fully displayed. By making the crucified Messiah the content of saving wisdom, Paul declares that to know God truly is to know Christ crucified — and that the "weakness of God" on the cross (v.25) is stronger than all human strength.

26.synthesis: Paul turns the paradox on the Corinthians themselves: "Consider your calling… not many of you were wise according to worldly standards, not many were powerful, not many were of noble birth" (v.26). God chose "the foolish… the weak… the low and despised" to shame the wise and strong, "so that no human being might boast in the presence of God" (vv.27–29). They are in Christ Jesus, "who became to us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption" (v.30), so that, as it is written, "Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord" (v.31). The very makeup of the church proves God's pattern of choosing the lowly.
26.historical_context: Paul's sociological observation (v.26) matches the evidence: the Pauline churches were largely sub-elite — artisans, freedpersons, slaves, and modest household patrons — though spanning a wider social range than most ancient associations, which created the very tensions the letter addresses (e.g., the Lord's Supper, 1 Corinthians 11:17–22). God's choice of "the low and despised" (v.28) continues a biblical pattern from the election of Israel "not because you were more in number" (Deuteronomy 7:7) to the beatitudes' blessing on the poor. The closing citation (v.31) draws on Jeremiah 9:24, where the only legitimate boast is in knowing the Lord — Jeremiah's anti-boasting polemic now aimed at Corinthian status-rivalry.
26.literary_structure: This section applies the cross-paradox of vv.18–25 to the congregation's own composition: as God saves through a "foolish" cross, so he calls "foolish" and "weak" people (v.27). The threefold "God chose" (vv.27–28) hammers the point of divine initiative, and the purpose clause "so that no human being might boast" (v.29) names the goal. The Jeremiah citation (v.31) is the scriptural capstone of the whole argument from v.18, and the anti-boasting principle it states will govern Paul's treatment of every later Corinthian problem.
26.theological_application: God deliberately saves and calls in a way that excludes all human boasting (v.29): the believer brings nothing of worth — wisdom, power, pedigree — to which God responds, so all glory belongs to him. This both humbles the proud and dignifies the overlooked: the "despised" are precisely whom God chooses (v.28). The only proper boast is "in the Lord" (v.31) — to glory not in oneself or one's leaders but in what God has done in Christ, which is the cure for the whole Corinthian disease of self-congratulation.
26.christology: Verse 30 is a compact summary of salvation in Christ: he "became to us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption" — the true wisdom the Corinthians craved is a person, and in him believers receive their right standing (righteousness), their consecration (sanctification), and their liberation (redemption). Everything needed for salvation is found in Christ and received as gift, leaving no ground for boasting except in him (v.31). That Jeremiah's "boast in the LORD" is fulfilled by boasting in Christ (Jeremiah 9:24) again quietly identifies Jesus with the Lord of Israel.
